title: Bastarda and Secretary hand
materials: ruling pen or pencil for guide-lines
sources: Bastarda and Secretary hand documented in Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932) — check UK copyright (life + 70; Fairbank died 1982 — still in copyright). Use structural description only. Gothic scripts generally described in Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain.
notes: Bastarda is a hybrid Gothic-cursive hand used in 14th-15th century Europe. Secretary hand is the English administrative variant used from c.1500-1700, infamous for its difficult letterforms. Both derive from textura quadrata but introduce cursive loops and ascender serifs (looped ascenders, forked ascenders). Covers: nib angle (15-20° for bastarda; secretary hand is more idiosyncratic), key letterform differences from textura (ascender loops, the high-stroked e, the two-stroke r), the ambiguous Secretary e/o, practice sequence. Focus on the Bastarda alphabet first as it leads more naturally to Secretary.
